summary: In 1983, University of Wisconsin researchers published database benchmarks showing Oracle performed dramatically worse than competitors—sometimes 30-100x slower. Oracle's Larry Ellison reportedly tried to have the lead researcher fired, then banned benchmarking in Oracle's EULA. This spawned "DeWitt Clauses" that spread across the software industry, preventing customers from publicly comparing performance. The post argues this anti-competitive practice harms transparency and suggests creating anonymous benchmark markets to circumvent these restrictions.
key_points: 1983 Wisconsin benchmark showed Oracle was 30-100x slower than competitors on many operations
Larry Ellison allegedly tried to fire the researcher and added anti-benchmarking clauses to Oracle's EULA
Major commercial databases (Oracle, Microsoft SQL Server) now forbid publishing benchmarks; only IBM allows them
Anti-benchmarking "DeWitt Clauses" have spread from databases to compilers and cloud services
The practice prevents customers from making informed purchasing decisions based on actual performance data
money_quotes: Larry Ellison wasn't happy with the results and it's said that he tried to have DeWitt fired.
Over the years, many major commercial database vendors added a license clause that made benchmarking their database illegal.

This gives people ammunition for a meta-argument that IBM probably delivers better performance than either Oracle or Microsoft, since they're the only company that's not scared of people publishing benchmark results.
There's at least one cloud service that disallows not only publishing benchmarks, but even "competitive benchmarking", running benchmarks to see how well the competition does.
It would be worse to have public benchmarks than to have it be known that they're afraid of public benchmarks.
key_insight: When vendors prohibit benchmarking in their licenses, it's effectively an admission that their performance is so poor that public comparison would be more damaging than the reputational cost of appearing afraid of transparency.
